title: SpaceX Lands on Nasdaq Friday — After the Market Just Got Absolutely Wrecked
subtitle: The AI trade just got torched. Broadcom vaporized $300 billion. Nvidia got tossed. And now the most hyped IPO in history lands right in the middle of the wreckage.
summary: AI stocks just got obliterated — -4.18% on the Nasdaq. Broadcom alone erased $300B. Nvidia got thrown out with the bathwater. And Friday? SpaceX IPO. $75 billion. The timing couldn't be more chaotic.

image.caption: Wall Street traders prepare for a high-stakes week as CPI data, Oracle earnings, and the SpaceX IPO converge.

bodyHtml:
The AI trade just got smoked. Nasdaq -4.18% on Friday. Worst single-day beatdown since April 2025. $1.3 trillion gone in two sessions. This wasn't a pullback — this was a rout. When you wipe out the GDP of South Korea in 48 hours, something fundamental shifted.

Broadcom was the grenade. AI guidance that humbled the Street — not a miss, but a statement that made everyone recalculate. $300 billion in market cap vaporized. The entire semiconductor complex got dragged into the crater. Nvidia dropped 6% just for being in the same zip code. When momentum cracks, even the untouchables get tossed out the window.

The SOXX semiconductor ETF imploded -10.4% over two sessions. That's a move that happens less than 2% of the time. This wasn't profit-taking. This was a liquidation.

And now? The most hyped IPO in history lands Friday.

🚀 SpaceX. $75 billion. Nasdaq. Friday, June 12. This is the liquidity event of the decade. Every institutional portfolio manager on the Street is doing the same math right now: how much Nvidia do I trim to fund SPCX? How much Broadcom gets the axe? Some of the selling pressure you're seeing this week isn't conviction — it's portfolio math. Room has to be made for the biggest name to hit the tape since Facebook.

But here's the flip side that almost nobody is clocking yet.

If SPCX opens hot — and everything about the roadshow says demand is through the roof — the entire narrative pivots on a dime. "AI is dead" becomes "the next frontier just opened." Rocket Lab, Redwire, Intuitive Machines — the whole space and defense complex catches the sympathy bid. Animal spirits don't die. They just find new toys. And SpaceX at $75 billion is the shiniest toy the market has seen in years.

What survived the Friday massacre? Defense didn't flinch. RTX and Lockheed held the line while everything else burned. Small caps actually flexed relative strength. When money rotates from AI momentum into defense value mid-panic — that's not a rotation. That's a regime change. The boring money is becoming the smart money.

Oracle earnings drop this week too. Normally a snoozer. Not this quarter. Oracle's cloud infrastructure business has been quietly landing nine-figure AI deals under everyone's radar — the dark horse of enterprise AI. Strong cloud revenue would be desperately needed proof that enterprise AI demand is real, not just chipmaker hype. A miss, and the bears get loud. But if Larry Ellison delivers and SpaceX pops on Friday? That's the kind of one-two punch that turns a bloodbath week into a launching pad.

Here's the stat that matters: After 4%+ Nasdaq drops since 2010 — 15 of them — the market was higher a month later in 12 out of 15 cases. Average bounce: +6.2%. Friday's selling was disproportionately systematic — algos, vol-targeting funds, risk-parity desks that sell because volatility spiked, not because any thesis broke. Those forced sellers are mostly done. Monday morning futures are flat. The panic has stabilized.

The real question isn't whether we bounce. It's whether SpaceX ignites the next mania before the dust even settles. A hot debut in a high-imagination sector on a Friday after a bloodbath week? That's the kind of setup that separates tourists from traders.

SpaceX is the main character this week. Oracle is the undercard. Everything else is noise. Watch the semis Tuesday morning — if they stabilize, Friday was capitulation and the bounce is incoming. Then it's all about Friday at the Nasdaq. 🚀
